data.title: Restaurant Brands International: Defying Industry Weakness With Accelerating Sales
data.description:
So far, the US stock markets are holding on fairly firm ground in 2026 as investors' bullishness continues. Underneath the surface, however, we should be prepared for a rotation out of expensive, momentum-driven tech stocks and into underappreciated categories that have recently underperformed.

The restaurant sector, in my view, is ripe for a turnaround. Many investors have ditched the space on account of slowing comp sales but are ignoring a number of stronger-performing names in the group. Restaurant Brands International (QSR), the parent of Tim Hortons and Burger King, is among the stocks that are overdue for a rally. Up ~10% in choppy trading over the past year, RBI has actually achieved meaningful comp sales acceleration in a tough environment. While it has its challenges, the stock is worth buying on its recent dip from the mid-$70s.

I'm initiating Restaurant Brands at a buy rating. Over the last quarter, it has become increasingly evident that consumers are pulling away from fast casual but sticking with fast food names, and RBI may be a good way to capture that shift.

For overall context on the company, Restaurant Brands is composed of six reporting divisions. Its principal four brands are Tim Hortons, Burger King, Popeyes Louisiana Kitchen, and Firehouse Subs. A separate international division reports the company's revenue in these brands outside the U.S. and Canada. And a sixth division, Restaurant Holdings, represents Burger King, Popeyes, and Firehouse Subs locations reacquired from franchisees over the past two years.

Despite a reasonable valuation and a healthy dividend, RBI is experiencing improved performance, particularly in its heaviest-hitting brands, Tim Hortons and Burger King domestically, which we'll focus on next.

Tim Hortons is Showcasing Accelerating Comp Sales

With the only possible exception of Dunkin' Donuts, there are probably few global brands that are more synonymous with donuts and coffee than Tim Hortons. What draws us to RBI in particular is that despite a slowdown in global consumer spending and weaker comps at many restaurant chains, Tim Hortons is showcasing surprising strength.

In Q3, Tim Hortons achieved 4.8% comp sales growth systemwide. As a reminder, comp sales measures the revenue growth from existing store locations, excluding the contribution of newly opened or newly closed locations. Comps accelerated versus 2.7% growth for the YTD period and are up 2x versus 2.4% in the year-ago Q3.

This brand is most prominent in its home market of Canada, and here, management notes that Tim Hortons is outperforming the Canadian quick-service restaurant industry by 3 points in comps. There's likely an element of Tim Hortons being a cheap, quick comfort in an otherwise tough economy here. Management notes as well that grab-and-go breakfast menu items, like the recently launched Loaded Croissant, have been strong performance drivers.

But equally driving success in Tim Hortons' focus on boosting traffic during different dayparts, i.e., serving customers beyond just the mornings. The company notes that it has launched $8.99 dinner deals with great success. Again, it appears here that consumers are resonating with the company's value offerings, similar to what we're observing from peers like McDonald's.

Though Tim Hortons is only a fifth of worldwide systemwide sales, it represented 46% of Q3 revenue (a greater portion of these locations are company-owned, versus the franchises where overseas locations are mostly third-party owned), so Tim Hortons' brand momentum is of special importance to the company.

BK and International Are Seeing Sales Improvements as Well

But RBI's recent success goes beyond just its largest second. Its second most critical brand, Burger King, is showing signs of a revival as well.

As shown in the chart below, BK also achieved healthy 3.1% comp sales growth worldwide, with 3.2% growth in the U.S. Again, this showed acceleration versus 1.2% growth in the YTD period.

Burger King has been a big turnaround story for RBI. Several years ago, the company launched its "reclaim the flame" initiative, which involved investing in restaurant interiors and technology and experimenting with its menu. We note as well that the company has closed a number of underperforming locations strategically, which has led to -1.1% net location growth. Still, systemwide sales grew, while RBI's direct revenue from Burger King grew 7% y/y to $387 million.

In my childhood, I thought of the "Big 3" fast food burger chains as McDonald's (MCD), Burger King, and Wendy's (WEN) - in that mental order. Burger King's Q3 US comps outperformed McDonald's at 2.4% U.S. growth:

And it also outperformed Wendy's by a wide mile, as Wendy's declined -4.7%:

And if you're inclined to think deeper promotions are part of the reason for the sales recovery, think again: BK's operating margins have also improved. Adjusted operating income growth of 10% y/y outpaced revenue growth, representing a segment margin of 31.8% that improved 90bps y/y.

And across brands, the international segment saw the fastest comps growth at 6.5% y/y, speeding up versus the YTD growth of 4.5%.

Per CEO Joshua Kobza's remarks on the Q3 earnings call on the drivers of international success:

Our same-store sales outperformed the industry in several key markets. including France, the U.K., Spain and Germany. In France, performance strengthened with the successful launch of our Baby burger boxes in July, a shareable snacking platform that's been a big hit with our guests.

In September, we expanded our chef collaboration platform to the U.K. with the launch of the Gordon Ramsay Wagyu burger made with 100% British Wagyu beef, which drove strong engagement and sales. This quarter, we also leveraged our global scale with a cross-market promotion of Naruto, the popular anime series, which performed well across countries like Germany, Brazil and China."

Cheap Valuation Amid Consistent Growth Profile

Now we come to the crux of my buy thesis: despite the strength of RBI's recent results, which are clearly well above peer comp sales trends, RBI continues to trade at quite modest levels.

The company's long-term growth algorithm calls for 3.0% comp sales growth annually, which the company's consolidated 4.2% growth in Q3 exceeded - again, despite a tough macro. It's also expecting 8%+ annual operating income appreciation.

For FY26, Wall Street is expecting the company to deliver 4% sales growth and $4.02 in pro forma EPS (+9% y/y).

So at current share prices near $69, RBI is trading at just a 17.2x forward P/E, which is a touch below the S&P 500. It's not a value stock, but considering RBI's market share gains (as evidenced by comps well outpacing peers), I'm more than happy to pay a high teens earnings multiple for quality and consistency.

Q4 Look-Ahead and Risks

RBI is due to report Q4 results in late January. To me, the items to watch out for are comp sales trends. We're hoping for comps to at least remain at the same pace as in Q3 and for the gap to other fast food peers to widen. We think there's opportunity for share grab as well as consumers rotate away from fast casual (the so-called "slop bowl" chains like Chipotle (CMG) and CAVA (CAVA)). Also, we're hopeful for an update about the company's location expansion plans for calendar 2026, especially given the company's recent commentary about wanting to achieve meaningful location growth in Tim Hortons while it's building on recent strength.

Of course, there are risks that we need to be mindful of here. The macro remains a big concern, and while it appears that consumers have gravitated toward fast food companies' value offerings recently, this trend could shift abruptly. Also, there are secular risks at hand: healthier eating is more in than ever, which could pressure the entire fast food category in the long haul.

The other risk is RBI's debt mound. Especially after investing in the overhaul of Burger King, the company is sitting at a materially high leverage ratio. Net debt sits at $2.9 billion, which is a trailing leverage ratio of 4.4x. Commercial banks typically consider 3x as the normal threshold for a highly leveraged business.

That said, we do note that the company's comps growth is translating into meaningful free cash flow expansion. YTD FCF of $996 million is up 11% y/y, so the company has the potential to deleverage fairly quickly.

Key Takeaways

All in all, the restaurant sector appears due for a rebound, especially for the stronger-performing names like RBI in the group. The stock's current ~3.5% yield also offers extra incentive for patience. Stay long here and bank on a rebound.

data.title: Rates Spark: Dutch Pensions Transition And A Fed Attack
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist; Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist; and Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

The second-largest Dutch pension fund published new details about their hedging strategy, challenging speculative positions for steeper curves. Expect more volatility from the very long end. We also reflect on the fallout from the remarkable attack on the Federal Reserve over the weekend, and how it might pan out ahead.

Reflections on Federal Reserve interference and the markets

Note that Chair Powell is not being hauled into court just yet. There is a grand jury proceeding to get through first. This is the precedent to the case getting to court. Note that the grand jury could also decide that there is no case to answer, and then that would be the end of the story; no court case.

Typically, though, the grand jury has quite a low bar to surpass to get a case to an actual court, but it is still a bar that needs to be surpassed. The grand jury proceeding could take weeks, and if we get through that, an actual case could take months. This will run and run, and Chair Powell, it seems, is not for caving.

This, in part, is why the market reaction has been very muted. The other part-rationale, as summarised here, reflects the basic mathematics that we are dealing with. If Chair Powell were removed, and Lisa Cook were removed, and we got one retirement morphed into a 'MAGA dove' in 2026, we would then have a total of four MAGA doves (including Stephen Miran).

That's still a minority. The total FOMC is 12, so two-thirds would not be MAGA doves. They would remain well short of an FOMC takeover. The President clearly wants Chair Powell to vacate his seat. If he does, it's still just one additional vote. Given that, the odds are that monetary policy does not get driven by the White House. Impacted, yes. But not dominated by.

But from a sentiment perspective, it's still a big deal. Attacking the Fed Chair like this is practically unprecedented. And in the extreme, dramatic curve steepening from both ends (with longer tenor yields up), and a weaker US dollar are likely consequences, should things really unravel. Still not our base view. But as a risk case view, it's rising in probability.

Dutch pension funds driving long end euro rates

Euro rates once again showed how domestic drivers can dominate global factors and Dutch pension funds are the ones moving markets this time. The second-largest Dutch pension fund (PFZW, €250bn AUM) published a more detailed outline of their new hedging strategy, which triggered a significant curve flattening on Monday.

In this updated plan, interest rate hedges will be 20% for younger participants and 100% for those in retirement. These ratios are likely significantly higher than markets anticipated. To put the numbers in perspective, ABP (the largest fund, aiming to transition in 2027) published hedging plans of 10% for younger people and 75% for elderly ones.

In effect, this means fewer fixed receiver swaps will have to be unwound, reducing the impact from the transition. We estimate that a 10 percentage point higher hedging ratio for PFZW translates to an increased demand for fixed receiver swaps of about €40m DV01.

Going forward, we expect more volatility from the Dutch pension fund transition. In the near term, we may see more flattening pressures as speculative positions get challenged. As pension flows start materialising, however, the overall net impact should still be steeper curves.

Tuesday's events and data view

The main release on Tuesday will be the US CPI. November's data was likely skewed by the late data collection given the government shutdown. Reverting to normal collection means our economists see some upside risk to the December figure, with a 0.4% month-on-month core rate that would lift the year-on-year figure to 2.8%.

Other data to watch are the ADP weekly data for the jobs market as well as the NFIB's small business sentiment indicator. Public comments by the Fed's Williams, Musalem and Barkin are likely to receive more scrutiny after the latest onslaught on Fed independence. Of note elsewhere are speeches from the UK's Governor Bailey and the ECB's Kocher.

Primary markets remain busy. The €SSA market kicked off the week with a €7bn, two-tranche deal from the EFSF (3y and 10y) and a €1bn 10y deal from Île-de-France. For Tuesday, the EU is lined up with a new 3y and 30y tap, while French CADES mandated a 7y social bond. While spreads are still tighter versus the end of last year, Monday showed signs of softness as spreads widened between 1bp to 2bp across the sectors.

In the govie space, Greece will be in the market for a new 10y benchmark. This comes alongside scheduled auctions from Austria (10y and 30y taps), Italy (new 3y) and Germany (new 5y). Elsewhere, the UK sells 10y gilt linkers and the US Treasury will auction US$22bn in 30y bonds.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Coca-Cola FEMSA: Reasonable Price, Solid Yield, And Venezuela Upside
data.description:
Coca-Cola FEMSA (KOF) is the world's largest independent Coca-Cola (KO) bottling franchise. It serves 78.7 million consumers in Mexico, along with approximately 200 million more consumers across the remainder of Latin America.

The company has long been part of Mexico's Fomento Economico Mexicano "FEMSA" (FMX) industrial conglomerate. FEMSA retains ownership of just under half of KOF's total outstanding stock and has a majority of the voting rights. This has been a beneficial relationship, as FEMSA operates its OXXO convenience store chain with more than 20,000 locations in Mexico and several thousand more around the rest of Latin America. This gives significant synergies in terms of branding and logistics for the distribution and sale of Coke-branded products within FEMSA's OXXO stores.

Both the parent FEMSA's shares and those of its Coca-Cola FEMSA subsidiary have delivered disappointing price performance over the past decade. However, these stocks have started to rally more recently amid a broader Latin American bull market. The Mexican stock market, as measured by the iShares MSCI Mexico ETF (EWW), has now nearly tripled since the March 2020 lows. That provides a solid tailwind for Mexican equity index components such as KOF stock.

Additionally, I believe the economies where the company operates, such as Mexico and Colombia, should benefit from broader economic developments such as the reshoring movement along with higher commodity prices. In addition, the recent developments in Venezuela present another potential upside catalyst.

Favorable Operating Results Trajectory

Coca-Cola FEMSA reported its Q3 results in October. They came in well ahead of expectations, with EPS of $1.52 easily topping the consensus estimate of $1.34. This continued a generally strong operational run for the company.

Though volumes were around flat for Q3, the company was able to grow operating income by nearly 7% thanks to increased prices and disciplined expense control. The Mexico market was weakest, with the company blaming a soft labor market and a drop in remittances as leading to slower consumer spending. However, this was offset by strength in other parts of LatAm. Brazil was a particular bright spot, as unit volumes increased nearly 3%, driven by market share gains.

Zooming out, analysts see the company's full-year 2025 revenues, as measured in dollars, jumping by 17.5%. A significant chunk of that has been driven by the appreciation of the Mexican peso and other LatAm currencies against the dollar over the past year. But organic growth is also adding to the momentum.

Analysts see revenues ticking along with 6% growth expected in 2026, and EPS in that year is projected to climb by 11% to $6.69 per share. That adds up to a perfectly reasonable 15x P/E multiple based on projected 2026 earnings.

While Coca-Cola FEMSA's share price remains far below its all-time highs set in the early 2010s, its EPS has significantly surpassed the prior peak and has shown a strong uptrend since 2021:

As a side note, as the chart shows, the company suffered a big loss at the time it deconsolidated its Venezuelan operations, and that speaks to potential upside if that asset becomes a significant contributor once again.

We can see the company's undemanding valuation on an EBITDA basis as well. Even after the recent rally, shares are still selling for just 8.3x EBITDA, which is well below the 15-year historical median:

In addition to the value proposition, there's also a strong income component as well.

Like many Latin American companies, Coca-Cola FEMSA does not have a fixed quarterly dividend payout. Rather, the dividend tends to fluctuate based on recent levels of profitability along with changes in exchange rates.

Regardless, Coca-Cola FEMSA has paid at least $2.47 per share in annual dividends in each of the past five years. That figure rose to nearly $4/share in 2025, and I'd expect it to rise again in 2026 as profitability has increased and the peso has appreciated against the dollar in recent months.

While Coca-Cola FEMSA's yield is variable, shares are usually good for at least a 3% annual payout, and it's been considerably higher than that over the past five years:

I'm not buying this stock just for, or even primarily for, the dividend. I think shares can head back to $150 each over the next two or three years. However, I do like getting a starting 3.9% yield as a kicker while the bullish thesis plays out.

Coca-Cola FEMSA: Venezuela's Coke Bottler

Coca-Cola FEMSA has long served as the Coca-Cola bottler for the nation of Venezuela. This used to be a significantly profitable part of the company's overall business back when the Venezuelan economy was performing better.

However, the Venezuelan economy ceased to function normally during the mid-2010s. Amid hyperinflation and sustained issues in obtaining inputs to manufacture products, foreign consumer packaged goods companies, including Coca-Cola FEMSA, have struggled to maintain even the semblance of normal operations.

A recent Seeking Alpha news article described that:

For its part, Coca-Cola is still present in Venezuela through a local bottler tied to Coca-Cola FEMSA, although the business has been deconsolidated and operates in a constrained, high-risk environment. Coca-Cola FEMSA holds an investment, referred to in the company's financial reporting as KOF Venezuela.

The U.S. campaign against President Nicolás Maduro and the increased U.S. involvement in Venezuela could set the stage for PepsiCo (PEP) and Coca-Cola to see enough improvement in supply chain management, plant modernization, and currency stabilization over the medium term to reset their strategies in the once key Latin American market.

What sort of impact could this have for Coca-Cola FEMSA and its investors in coming years?

In the company's 2024 integrated report, it noted that it currently serves about 276 million consumers across Mexico, Central America, and South America:

That figure, it appears, does not include Venezuela, which is currently treated as a non-consolidated operation.

As such, bringing Venezuela's roughly 30-35 million people back into the company's primary addressable market could possibly add more than 10% to topline revenues and earnings over the next few years if the Venezuelan economy reopens for business as usual.

That's not a huge figure on its own, but keep in mind that most consumer food and beverage companies have been struggling to show any meaningful growth at all in recent years. A potential low double-digit bump in revenues thanks to the renewed market opportunity in Venezuela could make KOF stock stand out from its food and beverage peers.

As a final note here, I'd highlight that Coca-Cola FEMSA has a broad and diversified geographic reach. I've seen some investors seem to think this is primarily just a Mexican company, and indeed it is headquartered there. But the company has an extensive footprint in Colombia, Brazil, and Guatemala, along with smaller presences in a number of other Latin American countries as well. As a result, I think people may be assigning too much Mexican political risk premium here; there's a lot of revenue coming in from other countries and currencies beyond Mexico.

KOF Stock Bottom Line

I've been eyeing KOF stock for several years, waiting for a potential catalyst that would make the stock break out of its decade-long slump:

The stock peaked back in 2013, at the height of the last Latin American boom. And, perhaps not coincidentally, the wheels fell off the Venezuelan economy right around then as well, taking away one of the company's larger markets at the time.

Now Latin American equities are in a new bull market, and Venezuela is potentially heading back toward a business-as-usual scenario. Both Colombia and Brazil, two significant Coca-Cola FEMSA markets, could see improved sentiment as well tied to their upcoming presidential elections later this year.

Meanwhile, KOF stock, which hasn't traded above $100/share since 2014, is now pushing right up against that resistance level. I think there's a good chance it breaks through the $100 barrier this time around. That makes me bullish on KOF stock for 2026. And in the bigger picture, I wouldn't be surprised to see 18-20x earnings on roughly $8/share of earnings power over the next few years, which argues for a longer-term price target around $150.

data.title: Traders Are Moving Past Powell's Investigation: U.S. Stock Index Outlook And Pre-CPI Trading Levels
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The morning session was nothing short of volatile. Overnight futures took a hit following news of the DoJ's investigation into Jerome Powell, once again casting a shadow over the Federal Reserve's independence. Yet, this market - and its traders - should never be underestimated.

Stocks caught a bid immediately after the open as participants shrugged off the headlines, using the early drop as yet another buying opportunity. Despite modest percentage gains on the day, the technical achievements are significant: the Dow is pacing for another record close, the Nasdaq is breaching monthly highs, and the S&P 500 is trading at fresh all-time highs.

Individual names are also making history, with Google (Alphabet) (GOOG) (GOOGL) recently breaking the $4T market cap milestone as Apple (AAPL) officially announced that they would require Google services for Apple Intelligence.

It seems "bad news is good news" in this environment. As long as the investigation remains a headline rather than an official indictment, investors remain resolutely bullish. Supplemented by heightened geopolitical pressures boosting defense and industrial sectors and driving metals to new highs, the trend remains intact.

However, expect momentum to potentially taper off as the day progresses; traders will likely begin squaring positions ahead of tomorrow's massive inflation (CPI) release at 8:30 AM (expected at +0.3% to 2.7% Y/Y)

Let's dive into our daily intra-session charts and NFP trading levels for the major US indexes: Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Healthcare is hurting once again, seeing quite some outflows in the beginning of this session after a huge 2025 trading. Financials are also struggling in the session as traders await key earnings for JPMorgan (JPM) and Bank of New York Mellon (BK) tomorrow, while energy drags from Exxon's (XOM) CEO comments on "uninvestable" Venezuela projects. The rest of the market shows green.

Dow Jones 2H Chart

Up a strong 1.16% since its open, the Dow is on pace to break new records again, now evolving within a strong intraday upward channel. A huge bull candle with no wicks is showing bull domination ahead of CPI, powered by its consumer defensive and machinery sectors - Walmart (WMT) is dominating the charts, up 3.70%.

  • A soft report tomorrow could easily send the Dow to 50,000.
  • A strong report, however, would hurt rate cut prospects and could lead to a retest of major support (the top candidate being 48,600 and 2H 200-MA).

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • 49,650 to 49,670 Current ATH Resistance
  • 46,670 All-Time Highs
  • 50,000 Potential Psychological Resistance

Support Levels

  • 49,324 2H 50-Period MA
  • 49,000 Psychological Pivot
  • 48,400 to 48,800 Major Support (2H MA 200 at 48,670)
  • Psychological Support at 48,000
  • 45,000 psychological level (main support on higher timeframe)

Nasdaq 2H Chart

After double top threats and sluggish price action throughout the past months, the Nasdaq is taking the lead again, brought up by the strong AI stock performance today:

Broadcom (AVGO), Oracle (ORCL), Google, a rebounding Nvidia (NVDA) and many others are pushing the tech heavy index to new highs, also breaking the double-top formed in the past week.

Still, traders will need to be cautious regarding short-timeframe bearish divergences. Tomorrow's NFP will be key to monitoring whether today's move fakes out or materializes into a real break.

  • Clearing the CPI, staying above 26,860 would point to a test of the October all-time highs.
  • Failing to stay above current resistance could lead to a test of the 25,000 level (25,100 is a key level to watch in this event; things would look bearish below).

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Intermediate resistance: 25,700 to 25,850 (breaking?)
  • Session highs: 25,877
  • All-time high resistance zone: 26,100 to 26,300
  • Current ATH: 26,283 (CFD)

Support Levels

  • 25,646 2H MA 50
  • Pivot 25,500 +/- 75 pts
  • Main Support: 25,000 to 25,250
  • 24,500 Main support
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 2H Chart

The S&P 500 went on to break its past day record highs yet again, with the new highs set at 6,986. Similarly to the Nasdaq, traders will need to be cautious regarding a so-so optimistic divergence forming as momentum slows during the ascent.

To mitigate the slow rise, however, a consistent uptrend sustains overall resilience in the market - the S&P could hold well a disappointing CPI tomorrow. Of course, it will depend on how bad it is (if it is).

A bullish CPI could well launch the S&P 500 beyond 7,000: 7,040 and 7,080 are potential profit-taking levels for the index.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • ATH Resistance: 6,945 to 6,975
  • Current All-Time High: 6,973
  • 1.382% Fib-Extension potential resistance at 7,001

Support Levels

  • 6,913 4H 50-MA Support
  • 6,800 Psychological Pivot and Range lows
  • Support 6,720 to 6,750 and 8H MA 50
  • 6,400 major psychological support

Safe trades and good luck for tomorrow's NFP!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: Another Blowout Earnings Look Inevitable
data.description:
It appears that buying Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) stock with a Strong Buy rating from me before the last earnings release was a good decision because the stock has delivered a total 9% return since then. The company releases its Q4 earnings this week (January 15th), and I think it is a good time to update coverage with the upcoming earnings preview.

I believe that TSM is poised to deliver another strong quarter because AI tailwinds are still strong with momentum accumulating. The company has a proven record of being able to capitalize on AI tailwinds, which is evident from strong revenue growth and margin expansion over the previous several quarters. Besides stellar fundamentals, there is a flawless earnings surprise record spanning the previous 12 quarters, accompanied by robust share price momentum. I am confident that TSM remains a Strong Buy into Q4 earnings.

TSM Q4 Earnings Preview

TSM has gained almost 51% in 2025, by far outperforming the S&P 500 Index (SPX). The stock's year-to-date performance in 2026 is also very confident, with a 6.5% share price increase compared to SPX's 1.8%. Overall, TSM's share price momentum is very strong with a 103% share price increase over the last nine months. TSM's robust share price momentum is also evident from the 14-day RSI indicator, currently sitting at 65. Such robust share price momentum is justified by TSM's rock-solid fundamentals.

As the world's largest semiconductor foundry, Taiwan Semiconductor is one of the major beneficiaries of the ongoing AI revolution. The company delivered 30%+ year-over-year revenue growth over the last six quarters, by far outpacing the last five years' 22.9% top line CAGR.

The crucial moment is that TSM is able to exercise strong pricing power due to soaring demand, as its key operating profitability ratios has increased aggressively. Over the same six quarters, the gross margin expanded by more than 600 basis points, while the operating margin increased by more than 800 basis points. Aggressive revenue growth combined with rapid profitability ratios growth confirms my assertion that TSM is extremely firmly positioned to capitalize on AI tailwinds.

For the upcoming quarter's earnings release, Wall Street analysts expect the trend of profitability outpacing revenue growth to continue. TSM's Q4 revenue is expected to increase by 22.9% year-over year, while the adjusted EPS is estimated to be up by 31.7%. TSM's record of double beats over the last twelve quarters suggests that the company is strong in delivering positive earnings surprises. Over the last sixteen quarters, there were zero EPS misses and only two negative revenue surprises. TSM's earnings surprise record over the last four years is almost flawless, which significantly increases the probability of another confident double beat on January 15.

Besides strong earnings surprise history, there are "more fundamental" reasons to believe that TSM's Q4 earnings release will be strong. The main reason is that AI tailwinds remain strong, which means more orders for TSM. Foxconn (FXCOF), another key manufacturer in the global AI supply chain, recently reported a strong 22% year-over-year revenue growth in Q4 2025. The company also reported that its December 2025 sales surged by 31.8% year-over-year, citing strong demand for AI-related electronics. TSM, itself, also recently reported strong 20.4% year-over-year revenue growth in December 2025.

Another evidence of AI revolution building its momentum is the recent presentation of Nvidia (NVDA) at CES 2026. Colette Kress, Nvidia's CFO, explicitly stated that Nvidia's previously cited $500 billion backlog through 2026 "has definitely gotten larger." She said that demand continues growing as customers are shifting toward agentic AI, which requires more computing power. Among potential demand drivers for NVDA in 2026, Colette Kress also mentioned China, as there is massive demand from this country's tech giants as well.

TSM is well positioned to further capitalize on AI tailwinds not only because of its strong recent historical performance but also because of its immense financial power. The company's tangible book value per share increased by almost 27% over the first three quarters of 2025. It is also vital to mention that TSM's net cash position increased significantly as well, from around $42 billion to $56.5 billion. Having such a strong financial position is a formidable asset for TSM because the industry is so demanding from the technological standpoint and the company needs to invest billions in R&D and new manufacturing facilities.

Valuation Analysis

Besides stellar fundamentals and highly likely blowout Q4 earnings, TSM is also attractively valued. With such strong AI tailwinds behind its back and TSM's status of the world's major semiconductor foundry, continued aggressive revenue and EPS growth looks inevitable. As a result, we see that TSM's estimated forward P/E ratio for FY2027 is expected to dip below 20. This is an extremely bullish setup, in my opinion.

TSM's compelling undervaluation is also confirmed by its extremely low non-GAAP PEG ratio of 1.23. As the table below suggests, it is almost 30% lower than the sector median. This is a massive gap, which does not look fair given TSM's aggressive revenue growth and rapidly expanding profitability.

Finally, Wall Street analysts also believe that TSM is notably undervalued. The average target price is $360, which indicates approximately 11% upside potential.

Mitigating Factors

One of the biggest headlines of 2026 are the military operation of the United States in Venezuela and large protests in Iran. While Venezuela and Iran are thousands of miles away from Taiwan, these developments increase the overall global geopolitical uncertainty. Moreover, we all know that there are historical tensions between Taiwan and China. I am not sure whether China will ever conduct a military operation against Taiwan because of the latter's historical political ties with the U.S. However, China held military drills around Taiwan just recently, which might mean they are preparing for future escalation. I think that probability of such a scenario is very low, but the potential adverse magnitude will be huge if the risk materializes.

If we speak about the upcoming earnings release itself, I believe that the key risk is the market's unpredictable reaction. I have a high level of conviction that TSM will deliver a strong quarter with an optimistic outlook. However, it might not be sufficient to impress the market because expectations around AI revolution are already quite aggressive. For example, Nvidia's latest earnings release as of November 19th was stellar, with many bullish forward-looking comments from CEO Jensen Huang. However, Nvidia's share price has been stagnating since then. Therefore, there might be muted reaction even to stellar earnings.

Conclusion

I continue buying TSM before the upcoming earnings release because I am confident that the company will deliver strong Q4 performance with the management's positive outlook for FY2026. Valuation remains compelling, meaning that TSM is a Strong Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: loanDepot: A 'Buy' With External And Internal Tailwinds (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've become bullish on loanDepot, Inc. (LDI).

LDI will benefit from the "Government-Sponsored Enterprises/GSEs" purchase of "Mortgage-Backed Securities/MBS" and potentially new investment restrictions for "Single-Family Rentals/SFRs."

Internally, I'm impressed with its in-house servicing loan strategy and the willingness to embrace AI.

My prior March 17, 2024, write-up touched on its worse-than-anticipated 4Q23 showing and a possible 2H24 upturn.

Fannie And Fred Are Enlarging Their Balance Sheets

A Jan. 9, 2026, SA News piece highlighted that Trump is "instructing unnamed representatives to acquire $200B in mortgage bonds." The article also mentioned the president's earlier remarks on how "he'll take steps to ban large institutional investors from buying single-family homes." I will discuss the first development here, and address the other event in the next section.

I think the purchase of MBS by GSEs will be good for LDI.

The $0.2 trillion is a meaningful amount. That's roughly the same as Baird's projected "net supply of new mortgages" for this year cited in a Dec 18, 2025 AAM commentary.

Last week, "the 30-Year Fixed Mortgage Rate" fell "below 6%" for "the first time since February 2023" according to SA analyst "Hoya Capital's" Jan. 11 update.

My opinion is that LDI's prospects have become more promising, thanks to lower rates and tighter mortgage spreads.

The firm drew attention to its transformation from "a de novo start-up in 2010 to at one point becoming the second-largest retail lender in the country" at its 3Q25 briefing.

UBS Group's (UBS) January 9 research note (not publicly available) titled "Implications Of The $200B MBS Purchase Directive" quantified the effects of the rate environment changes.

The incremental refinance volume brought by Fannie's and Fred's moves is estimated to be $0.45 trillion, based on UBS's calculations. That assumes a 6ppts (percentage points) rise in the proportion of "the market in-the-money."

LDI's 10-Q filing indicated that its "loan refinancing volumes" are "primarily driven by fluctuations in mortgage loan interest rates."

SFRs Could Become Off-Limits For Wall Street

Individuals may no longer have to compete with REITs and PE firms in the single-family space if the president gets his way as outlined in the preceding section.

Morgan Stanley (MS) issued a report (inaccessible to the public) named "Thoughts On SFRs" on Jan 8 assessing this possibility. MS is forecasting "$40 billion of new mortgage originations", implying a "3% increase in annual US purchase originations."

The institutions don't take up mortgages from LDI or its peers to finance their single-family purchases. They have alternative funding sources such as bonds or revolvers.

You can imagine that keeping PEs/REITs out of this segment will translate into greater mortgage-related demand from occupiers. MS's expectations of enhanced origination growth are realistic in my view.

Things can get even better for LDI in certain scenarios.

The sell-side has speculated on the likelihood of some REITs being "forced to liquidate or decided to liquidate (their single-family properties) due to an inability to scale their portfolios."

In the case above, the housing inventory additions could depress prices and attract individual homebuyers back to the market. LDI will most probably witness more loan inquiries if that happens.

Spotlight On Self-Help Measures

LDI isn't solely reliant on regulations or policies to improve its future performance.

At its 3rd quarter call, it stressed the importance of exploiting "positive operating leverage." Its goal is to enjoy "both the benefit of increased volume and increased margin" when "volumes return."

LDI's corporate slides draw attention to its "strong recapture rate at 65%," and a "$0 customer acquisition cost" for new business from its existing borrower base.

The firm revealed at the Q3 meeting that it "made an investment to bring servicing in-house" in '20. That has boosted its top line and bottom line through low attrition and optimized marketing expenses, respectively.

It has taken other actions to support its sales. These include sponsoring sports events (e.g. MLB/NHL) and working closely with property developers for referrals.

Separately, "Artificial Intelligence/AI" has turned into a critical profitability lever.

One example is the automation of LDI's call centers, so that fewer human operators are needed.

Management's 3Q25 briefing comments suggest that it's also "pivoting the use of new and emerging technologies across sales, operations, and software engineering" to "lower our cost of production."

The market sees LDI reversing from a net loss of $0.06 billion in FY25 to register +$56M earnings for FY26 (source: S&P Capital IQ). This is consistent with the multiple tailwinds described in this write-up.

You Should Be Mindful Of These Risks

The SFR-related ban might not materialize if Trump's proposal is challenged in court.

LDI may not execute well on internal initiatives, causing the actual revenue boost or margin expansion to be less substantial than hoped for.

My Concluding Remarks

LDI's rating is revised from "Hold" to "Buy."

I assign a target P/B of 3.4 times to this name. That implies a +42% upside (3.4/2.4), considering its current valuation ratio of 2.4x.

I've used the "Gordon Growth Model." My inputs are a 21% ROE (FY27 consensus), a +3% growth (mature economy proxy), and an 8.3% cost of equity (financial services sector average).

As per my analysis, there are favorable developments within and outside the company, which can act as re-rating triggers.

data.title: FTA Aviation Series C Preferred Shares Remain A Hold Despite Juicy 8.1%
data.description:
I covered FTAI Aviation’s Preferred Series C shares (NASDAQ:FTAIN) with a hold rating in my last report. The stock price has remained flat since then with a 1.8% total return compared to the S&P 500’s 7.9%. So, you would have been better off putting the money into an S&P 500 tracker, but at the same time the preferred shares are more of a fixed income idea for which I believe comparisons to the S&P 500 performance are not necessarily meaningful. It should be noted that interest rate environments and the possibility of redemption of the shares plays a substantial role. In this report, I discuss whether FTAI Aviation Preferred Series C shares are currently more attractive with a focus on the interest rate environment and how possible redemption of shares limits upside which leads me to maintain my hold rating.

About The FTAI Series C Preferred Series C Yields 8.1%

FTAI Series C Preferred Stock was issued in March 2021 with a par value of $25, generating an annual dividend of $2.0625 paid quarterly. Currently, the stock is trading at $25.43, bringing the effective yield to 8.1%. This is unchanged from my prior report.

The preferred shares are redeemable on or after June 2026. The shares have a cumulative dividend, which means that if FTAI Aviation misses a dividend payment, the dividend still has to be paid eventually. However, so far, FTAI Aviation has not missed a single dividend payment to preferred shareholders. The shares can be redeemed before 2026 in the event FTAI Aviation is downgraded by all rating agencies.

Redeeming The Preferred Shares Remains The Base Case Scenario

The yield of more than 8% does look attractive, but given that the stock is redeemable there are some concerns on share price appreciation. The Series C yield is structured as a fixed-rate reset nature. In June 2026, the yield will be remeasured against the five-year Treasury rate plus a 7.378 point spread.

Currently the 5-year Treasury rate is 3.75%. If current interest rates do not drastically change, the yield would be 11.1%. Previously, the five-year treasury yield was below the fed fund rate. That is no longer the case. The fed fund rate is currently 3.64% which is 11 basis points below the 5-year Treasury rate. With a relatively small spread between the two, it means that cuts are expected but not in an aggressive way.

The current expectation is that by June 2026, when the Preferred Series C has its rate reset, the fed fund rate will be around 325-350 basis points. This is unchanged from my prior assessment. If we take the mid-point of the target range, we get to 3.375%. Together with the 11 basis points spread, it would imply a five-year treasury rate of around 3.5%. Adding the 7.378 points spread, we would get to a new yield of 10.86% indicating a 10.7% effective yield using the current price of the preferred shares of $25.43.

Currently the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread indicates that investors are looking for a 2.76 points spread on top of the five-year treasury rate implying a 6.5% yield that investors are looking for. In the case of redemption, investors will likely get two more dividend payments of $0.5156 and at current prices there will be a $0.43 loss from redemption bringing the effective annualized yield to 4.8%. So, the Series C shares do not look particularly attractive.

Even if the company would consider going through the rate reset, we note that the lower interest rate environment is tightening spreads that investors are looking for and as a result rather than a prospective effective 10.7% yield it would make a lot of sense to redeem the stock as there is a substantial spread between the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread and the effective yield FTAI’s preferred stock would offer.

If the Fed modestly eases rates, there could be a 75-125 basis point spread between the fed fund rate. Taking the mid-point would give a 4.33% Treasury rate. This would send the yield to 11.7% next year. However, I believe that the 5-year Treasury rate will continue to signal expectations of rate cuts, and as a result, I would not be surprised if the yield would reset at 10%. Furthermore, we note that the company already called the Series B in 2025 and the Series A in 2024. So, redeeming the Series C lies in line with expectations.

Typical Spreads On 5-Year Treasury Support Redemption

FTAI Aviation stock has a BB+ rating since November 2025 while the preferred shares have a credit rating of BB-. For BB- preferred shares, a typical spread compared to the 5-year treasury rate would be 200-400 basis points. This indicates a 5.75%-7.75% yield. This would imply a price target between $27.50-$35.86. So, the current yield is substantially above what would be typical for BB- rated preferred stock and the prospective reset yield would also be substantially above that. All of this supports that redemption of the preferred shares is likely and simultaneously removes the upside to $27.50-$35.86 derived from a 5.75%-7.75% yield range. As a result, I deem the stock a hold.

Conclusion: FTAI Aviation Series C Preferred Stock Continues To Be A Hold

FTAI Aviation continues to offer a juicy 8% yield, but that is not a reason to buy the stock in my view at current prices as a possible redemption would put the yield at just 4.8%. The best course of action would actually be to continue holding the stock, collect the two remaining dividend payments and after that sell the stock if the price is still above $25 anticipating a redemption. If the price is below $25, continue holding the stock until redemption.

data.title: Gilead Sciences: From All-Time Highs To Higher Highs
data.description:
Since my article from October, "What To Expect From Gilead in Q3 2025", Gilead Sciences' stock (GILD) reached an all-time high of $128.70.

However, after November 20, a corrective pattern called a zigzag started to form. Currently, I expect it to end in mid-January. After which, according to my assessment, Wave 5 will resume upon reaching the +2σ band formed from the VWAP, namely $134.50.

And since I want to be as objective as possible, I ask myself: Why do I see Gilead as having an attractive risk/reward profile?

The launch of Yeztugo strengthened Gilead's HIV franchise

First of all, I want to note that Gilead actively captures the HIV treatment market, even without taking into account Yeztugo. I see this through sales of its three key drugs, Biktarvy, Descovy, and Yeztugo.

So, the big-selling HIV medication called Biktarvy generated $3.69 billion in revenue for Gilead in Q3, up 6.2% year-over-year, and beat my "base case" scenario by $65 million.

Returning to Yeztugo [lenacapavir], it was approved by the FDA and the EC on June 18 and August 26, respectively, as pre-exposure prophylaxis against HIV.

Sales of Yeztugo were $39 million in Q3, 2.6 times higher than the previous quarter.

And on December 3, CFO Andrew Dickinson expressed great confidence in lenacapavir at a healthcare conference organized by Citigroup (C). He also disclosed, to my delight, that Yeztugo sales are expected to be about $100 million in Q4.

A couple of things that I'd highlight our J-code for reimbursement in the U.S. came -- it was effective October 1. It came a couple of months earlier than expected. We hit 75% payer covered lives, and that's both across commercial as well as government payers.

A couple of months earlier than expected as well, which is great. And we guided to roughly $150 million of sales for the first half of this year, including a little less than $100 million of expected sales for the fourth quarter. So it's off to a great start.

This is just the beginning of what we expect to be kind of a steady, consistent, durable growth in this business in Yeztugo, in particular, over time.

So, I can call its launch extremely successful. And the secret behind this success lies in lenacapavir's competitive advantages over GSK's Cabenuva/Dovato (GSK) and Johnson & Johnson's Edurant (JNJ).

I already talked about them in the article "Gilead Sciences: HIV Leadership Outshines Decline in COVID-19 Drug Sales."

But to provide a more comprehensive picture of the HIV market, I've also added other drugs to the table below.

And a key milestone last year related to the lenacapavir development program was the publication on December 15 of the results from the Phase 3 ARTISTRY-2 study [NCT06333808].

Gilead reported that the combination of [BIC] bictegravir 75 mg, an integrase inhibitor and a component of Biktarvy, with [LEN] lenacapavir 50 mg was statistically non-inferior to Biktarvy.

And given that BIC+LEN met the primary endpoint, non-inferior to multi-tablet regimens, in the ARTISTRY-1 trial [NCT05502341], I believe this combination will be approved by the FDA in the first quarter of 2027.

GILD is not stopping there and is evaluating lenacapavir as HIV prevention with once-yearly injections or as a combo with Merck's Islatravir (MRK), monoclonal antibodies [teropavimab and zinlirvimab].

Sales of Gilead's Livdelzi grew more than I factored into my DCF model

Besides its HIV franchise, it has a diversified portfolio of drugs targeting liver diseases. One of these is Livdelzi [seladelpar], a PPAR-delta agonist.

Since the acquisition of seladelpar, as a part of the deal with CymaBay Therapeutics, in my view, it has become the preferred option among hepatologists for the treatment of PBC [primary biliary cholangitis].

I don't want to repeat all the reasons that led to the changes in the clinical practice to treat this autoimmune liver disease. But in short, Livdelzi is more effective in reducing pruritus, ALT, and alkaline phosphatase levels compared to Iqirvo's Ipsen/Genfit (IPSEY) (GNFTY) and Ocaliva. By the way, Alfasigma withdrew Ocaliva from the US and EU markets.

As a result, in Q3, sales of Livdelzi rose 28.2% quarter-on-quarter to $100 million versus Iqirvo's €48.6 million. Although I remind you that Iqirvo was launched three months earlier, meaning it had a first-mover advantage.

Also, CCO Johanna Mercier sounded pretty optimistic on the Q3 call when she talked about Livdelzi.

Livdelzi grew 35% sequentially, driven by strong commercial execution, including some new launches outside the U.S. and withdrawal of a competitor's product in the U.S. We are particularly pleased to see strong levels of persistence among users and believe Livdelzi shows clear differentiation and value to those with PBC.

Risks

And again, risks.

I have mixed feelings about Gilead's attempts to compete with Merck, AstraZeneca (AZN), and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) in the oncology market. It doesn't have the same experience in developing anti-cancer drugs as this 'trio'.

And even its acquisition of Kite Pharma for $11.9 billion back on August 28, 2017, hasn't brought Gilead any closer to its goals in this therapeutic area.

So, total sales of its two CAR T-cell therapies, Yescarta and Tecartus, fell by 10.9% YoY to $432 million in Q3.

In my opinion, the weakening demand for them reflects tough competition from more effective drugs for large B-cell lymphoma, such as Roche's Columvi (RHHBY) and Breyanzi.

Takeaway

Gilead started 2026 on a high note.

And according to Seeking Alpha, it will announce its Q4 figures next month, on February 10.

To my regret, Gilead itself has not yet specified the date, but has only informed investors that acquired IPR&D expenses will be $539 million in this quarter.

The consensus analyst estimate for GILD's EPS is $1.89, and revenue is $7.68 billion. However, I think Wall Street is again setting the bar low, as it has in eight of the last 11 quarters.

Overall, as a reminder.

Gilead's non-GAAP EPS reached $2.47 in Q3. It rose by 22.3% YoY and 22.9% QoQ, thanks to medications which I mentioned earlier, and also Vemlidy and Trodelvy.

And given the strong results from the iMMagine-1 Phase 2 trial published on December 6, in which anito-cel, developed jointly with Arcellx (ACLX), has demonstrated efficacy similar to J&J/Legend Biotech's Carvykti (LEGN) in the treatment of RRMM, I continue to cover Gilead with a 'Buy' rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Duolingo Has Plunged, But There's No Opportunity Here Yet
data.description:
I was looking through a list of the biggest losing stocks in recent months, and Duolingo, Inc. (DUOL) stood out to me.

I love consumer tech companies that can become a platform for a given space. I missed Netflix (NFLX), despite enjoying the service because I thought it was overvalued during its aggressive growth stage. However, I did not make the same mistake twice; I bought Spotify (SPOT) early in its days as a public company and enjoyed sizable upside there. I have also been quite bullish on Unity Software (U), which, I believe, can be a picks-and-shovels play for the video games space and capture a significant chunk of that TAM over the next decade.

With that context in mind, Duolingo is a similarly interesting company. In theory, it can grab a sizable piece of the language-learning market, and even adjacent learning spaces such as music, computer programming, and chess. Duolingo is reporting rapid revenue growth and has a well-known brand. And now, with DUOL stock down more than 50% over the past six months, shares are on sale compared to prior prices. So, what's not to like?

Valuation Remains Demanding

It's easy to look at a stock chart like Duolingo's with a steep plunge and think that it must be approaching buy-the-dip territory. And, after all, the stock is now selling at a reported just 20x forward earnings. That's a decent entry point, right?

But that P/E ratio is misleading. The company enjoyed a huge tax benefit last quarter, leading to Duolingo reporting $5.95 of earnings in Q3. This beat expectations by a whopping $5.19 per share. You don't get that level of earnings beat simply from stronger-than-expected operating results.

And indeed, the confounding factor here was that the company reported $246 million in gains related to income tax. By contrast, Duolingo only earned $46 million of income from operations during the quarter. Excluding the tax effect, Q3 EPS would have been approximately 93 cents per share. And that figure would be further reduced if you backed out share-based comp from the mix.

Putting stock-based compensation aside, on an adjusted earnings basis, here is what analysts are forecasting going forward:

Once the one-time tax effect rolls off, we're looking at about 40x adjusted forward earnings for 2026 and 33x earnings for 2027.

On an EBITDA basis, Duolingo remains exceedingly expensive:

Just because the stock used to trade at 250x EBITDA doesn't necessarily guarantee that it is a bargain at 57x EBITDA today.

Duolingo: A Gaming Company?

In a recent note, Bank of America suggested that there could be significant upside for Duolingo. The report argues that Duolingo is an entertainment company and that it has two addressable markets, one for learning and one for casual gaming. It goes so far as to suggest that Duolingo could become one of the world's top five mobile gaming apps, putting it in competition with Roblox (RBLX) and Candy Crush.

There is a grain of truth here. Duolingo is a lot more entertaining than earlier versions of language learning software. I was short language software company Rosetta Stone, back when it was a publicly traded entity, in significant part because I felt it was both ineffective in teaching languages and also not particularly engaging for users.

While Duolingo (in my opinion anyway) is not especially compelling in terms of how well it can actually teach a language, it is certainly one of the more entertaining apps in the learning space.

That said, I have kids. I sometimes let them borrow my tablet to play games for practicing math or writing skills. But, unless I keep a close eye on them, they'll end up playing some mobile game with no redeeming educational value rather than the learning one.

I simply don't believe that, for most users, Duolingo is sufficiently entertaining on its own to be true competition to lazy scrolling time that would otherwise be spent on Netflix, TikTok, or Candy Crush. For someone inherently motivated to learn languages, chess, or music, Duolingo is a suitable option. It's going to be more entertaining than reading a book or going to a college class that teaches that subject, sure. But is it a direct competitor to Roblox for pure pleasure-seeking mobile gaming experiences? I don't buy it.

I will grant that Duolingo is quite addictive; I know several people who have felt an almost compulsive need to keep their Duolingo streak going. It's certainly something to have users want to come back every day to keep a streak going. Just look at companies like Snapchat (SNAP) that have been able to harness that tendency for proof of this.

Yet, I fear that it is cheap engagement.

Wells Fargo recently downgraded Duolingo stock, arguing that the company's brand is weakening, and that users are rejecting Duolingo's more aggressive monetization efforts. One independent analyst also worked through Duolingo's year-in-review data and concluded that most users are spending just a couple of minutes a day or less on the app.

I would love to see Duolingo provide more granular information around its user behavior. Because, unless proven otherwise, my hunch is that many, if not most, users are active just long enough to keep their streaks going and aren't actually using the app for long periods of time regularly. This, in turn, implies that Duolingo could struggle to get a large portion of its free-tier users to eventually upgrade.

As an analogy here, I have a long daily activity streak in a puzzle game called Wordle. But I spend about one minute a day on that app and would never think of buying a subscription for it. A user can be active without being valuable.

And indeed, as of Q3 data, fewer than 10% of monthly active users pay for a Duolingo subscription, which seems like a fairly lackluster result for an app that bulls say has such high user engagement. Meanwhile, advertising makes up less than 10% of Duolingo's total revenues. The vast majority of the user base is contributing hardly anything to the company's bottom-line results, as they don't pay for a subscription, and ad revenues are not moving the needle for Duolingo.

Simply put, I don't think people are treating Duolingo like they would a highly pleasurable mobile game such as Candy Crush. If the company can't get a lot more paid subs or greatly increase ad revenues from free users, this growth story is not going to meet bullish expectations.

AI: Friend Or Foe?

Some bears believe Duolingo will quickly become irrelevant because of AI. In a world with perfect machine translation, what's the point of learning another language?

I can say that I started learning Spanish, as an adult, in 2010. Already, at that time, people told me I was wasting my time because we'd have perfect translators on our phones within five or ten years. It's now 2026, and while real-time phone translation has advanced greatly, it hasn't come anywhere close to making language learning irrelevant.

Phone translation is useful for tourists doing stuff like deciphering restaurant menus or street signs. But are people using real-time phone translation for more demanding activities such as doctor's appointments, legal functions, buying real estate, etc. in other countries? I've lived in Latin America for more than 10 years now, and I've hardly ever seen foreigners try to use phone translation for serious conversations or business. Folks still either learn the language or hire a person to interpret for them.

And even for everyday conversation, most people simply don't seem that inclined to try to talk to each other via phone translation to break through the language barrier. Maybe other countries and regions are different, but here in Latin America, I just don't see it in daily life. There's so much nuance to slang and local idioms, not to mention the body language and other non-verbal functions of communication that are lost with phone-based communication rather than verbally talking to each other.

Long story short, translation will keep getting better, but most people moving to another country or learning a language for work purposes aren't going to outsource that to a phone in the immediate future.

A bearish counterpoint, however, would be that many people learn languages (especially English) for work reasons, and a whole range of jobs associated with this could become obsolete. Think of people in emerging markets learning English to work in call centers, perform IT jobs, or serve tourists at hotels, restaurants, etc. It's not difficult to imagine much of that, particularly call centers, getting displaced by AI within a few years. In that case, many people will never bother to learn English in the first place if doing so no longer leads to decent employment prospects.

There's also the possibility that AI will allow other companies to code up better alternatives to Duolingo. The moat around software in general seems to be shrinking, and there's a ton of capital going after gamified versions of all sorts of things. Duolingo is a first mover here, but as development cycles get faster, Duolingo's edge could shrink.

Duolingo is leaning heavily into AI to improve its own courses and workflows. The company introduced dozens of new languages, for example, with lessons that were built in part with AI tools rather than human translators. That could be a good way to offer instruction for less popular languages that would otherwise fall through the cracks.

That said, there was dramatic consumer backlash as Duolingo applied more AI methods to its overall learning flow.

In April, Duolingo announced its new AI-first strategy, while also disclosing that it would reduce the number of humans that it employed. Here's Duolingo's stock price since the AI-first push was revealed to the public.

The company's engagement metrics almost immediately decelerated once the AI-first memo was published, and the stock price soon followed suit.

This came to light with the company's underwhelming Q2 earnings results. CEO Luis Alfonso von Ahn Arellano acknowledged that they underperformed expectations directly due to the AI backlash:

We came in at 40% (DAU growth), which is slightly on the lower end of what we thought. Everything is -- we feel very strong about this. The reason we came towards the lower end was because I said some stuff about AI, and this was -- I didn't give enough context. And because of that, we got, particularly in our social media, we got some backlash on it.

There are numerous software companies, Adobe (ADBE) being another great example, where you can make a perfectly valid case that AI will both harm and hurt their business, and it's a question of which effect will ultimately be greater. For Duolingo specifically, AI should make it easier to produce additional learning content. Additionally, in a more competitive job market, people need more skills to secure employment. This could drive more engagement with smart learning tools.

Duolingo's CEO has also used far-reaching descriptions of how AI tutors and app-based learning could overtake human teaching in the future. I'm quite skeptical this will happen anytime in the near term. But, if it, in fact, does become possible and Duolingo is the company to achieve this, it would likely mean tremendous returns for the company and its shareholders.

I don't believe AI is going to immediately make language learning obsolete, though it will reduce the number of casual learners who only want to pick up a few phrases before a vacation. It could meaningfully shrink the TAM longer-term, though, especially for folks learning English as a second language for career purposes. Duolingo could also suffer (additional) reputational damage from rolling out poorly designed AI tools or AI-related messaging in the future.

My overall view is that AI will be neutral to slightly unfavorable to Duolingo over the next five years. But the range of potential outcomes here is huge, and any opinion I have here can change at a moment's notice, given how quickly the technology is advancing.

Duolingo Stock Verdict

I get the bullish case here. There's the potential for this to become the Netflix or Spotify of the app-based learning space. Language learning is a large market, and even more so if Duolingo can carve out a decent profile in teaching other fields such as computer programming, music, etc.

Despite the company's recent AI messaging missteps, revenue growth remains strong. And the firm is meaningfully profitable and cash flow generative.

You can sketch out a realistic case where Duolingo continues to post 25%+ annualized revenue for at least a few more years and exceeds the market's current expectations. I don't think this is a high probability, mind you, but it's certainly within the range of plausible outcomes if Duolingo plays its AI cards right and can move into adjacent learning markets as well. And if the gaming argument is correct and Duolingo is actually the next Roblox, the sky is the limit for the business' growth profile.

The issue I have in underwriting this, however, is I see that full bullish case as only something like a 10%-20% probability outcome. Throw in another 20% chance that the stock does okay-to-reasonably well, and I'd guess that in the other 60%-70% of potential future scenarios, Duolingo badly trails the market. I'm not really keen on these sorts of moonshot investments, where I have a small chance of hitting a jackpot and a large chance of underperforming the market.

At $500/share earlier this year, I don't see how DUOL stock could have possibly lived up to expectations. Shares were priced beyond perfection at more than 250x EBITDA.

I can, however, imagine an outcome where Duolingo grows into a real $500 per share valuation over the next five years, in which case you could get roughly a triple from buying at today's less exuberant price. That'd be excellent.

But, if I had to bet, I think it's more likely that this stock is trading under $100/share five years from now than it is to be over $500. Be it AI disruption, newer and better apps coming along, a persistent decline in user engagement, or whatnot, there are various ways for Duolingo's growth thesis to fail.

And at a starting price of around 40x forward earnings, 50x EBITDA, and 7x revenues, there's little margin for safety here. As such, even after the sharp sell-off in recent months, the odds favor more downside ahead.

data.title: On Holding: Running Like Swiss Clockwork
data.description:
Recap

The Swiss premium sports brand On Holding AG (ONON) is up 29% since I first covered it here on Seeking Alpha back in May 2024, roughly in line with the S&P 500’s gain of 31%. My conclusion was clearly leaning positively on the back of excellent profitable growth but a high valuation, which could nevertheless have been justifiable given the growth dynamics. I came to this conclusion by crafting an integrated financial model and partly backwards-engineering market participants’ long-term growth assumptions.

After well over a year and a half, I am providing a simplified update regarding On’s still-outstanding positioning and execution.

Unlocking Multiple Growth Opportunities

On Holding has long communicated its two key growth opportunities: regional expansion and category expansion. Both are working out as hoped. The company percentage of sales in the Americas came down to 58% from 64% for the first nine months of the year. This was attributed to explosive growth of more than 100% in Asia-Pacific and dynamic growth in the 30s in EMEA. When we talk about growth around the 20s as the weakest region (Americas), we are certainly dealing with an excellent growth profile through and through.

Nevertheless, the continuously strong Swiss Franc weighs on all reported revenues, with the US-Dollar weakness causing the largest discrepancy between constant-currency growth and reported growth. Yet, On’s growth as of today is impressive enough to essentially offset currency headwinds.

Apparel and Accessories sales have been roughly doubling during the nine-month period, while Shoes grew around the 30% range. Here again, it looks impressive how despite continued dynamic growth in Shoes, their revenue share continues to drop in the direction of 90%, as Apparel and Accessories catch up so strongly, albeit starting from relatively low levels.

Regarding sales channels, too, there is no weakness in sight, with DTC slightly outgrowing wholesale but not significantly. The latest channel mix was approximately 60:40 wholesale:DTC. DTC growth reflects extraordinary brand strength, as Nike, on the other hand, had to soften its aggressive DTC push and re-establish wholesale partnerships in order to remain relevant and visible.

With that, On remains “one of the young new market participants with the potential to threaten even Nike in the long term,” as I wrote last time. As laid out in my recent Nike article based on Piper Sandler data, Nike has been losing significant US-teens’ mind share recently. In fact, On’s TTM revenue of $3.6 billion already represents 8% that of Nike’s $46.5 billion. This is an impressive run-up in relative market share to the market leader, as visible below. On the other hand, I am not at all writing off Nike, and any successful brand revitalization could dampen On’s growth story.

Improving Profitability

Please refer to my key financials overview below for the following:

A – After annual growth rates of at least 60% until 2023, top-line growth has briefly slowed to 20% in 2024 but has notably reaccelerated above 40% lately.

B – At the same time, On impressively boasts expanding gross margins solidly above the 60% mark with 62% on a TTM basis and an upwardly-revised target of 62.5% for the fiscal year. This is far above Nike’s gross margins in the 40s and underscores On’s strong premium standing. As such, On’s gross margin reality is also notably better than I had modeled in 2024, when I assumed it would gradually approach Nike’s.

C – On’s operating margin, with 12% on a TTM basis, is already in the realm of Nike’s best years and I continue to expect it to expand significantly through economies of scale as the company’s growth progresses. On the other hand, On might also reach a point where competitive headwinds might increase and must be offset by increased marketing spend.

D – Next, my assumption was that On’s strong internal financing power thanks to its profitability will notably decrease the need for shareholder dilution. Indeed, diluted share count has increased by only 1% annually over the last three years.

F – Free cash flow conversion from operating income has stabilized around or above the 100% mark during all of the past three years, indicating healthy maturing and scaling of the company. TTM FCF of $440 million represents a 2.8% yield against On’s current enterprise value of $15.7 billion, equivalent to a 36x EV/FCF multiple. Given the company’s growth, this multiple does not appear too demanding this time.

Continued: More On Valuation

I am not conducting a comprehensive DCF valuation this time, but I acknowledge the following changes are positively and negatively affecting discount parameters. First, raw company beta came down from a 24-month and 60-month average of 1.99 to 1.76, based on Seeking Alpha data, potentially enhancing the stock’s value. Second, international expansion might increase country risk, conversely weighing on valuation.

Compared with Nike, On already appears attractively valued on a forward basis. 2027 FWD P/E for On is 23x, while Nike’s is 28x. 2028 FWD P/E is 17x, while Nike’s is 23x.

Takeaway

On is firing so strongly on all cylinders that even significant FX headwinds due to a strong Swiss Franc cannot materially weigh on the operational growth dynamics. While still growing strongly in the Americas and with Shoes, EMEA, APAC and Apparel/Accessories expansion are the explosive growth drivers. With that, On rapidly captures relative market share to Nike, but competitive pressure is likely to increase with scale. On the other hand, operating profitability is even expected to improve with scale. Valuation appears fair against the backdrop of On’s growth dynamics. As a result, I remain positive. The Hold rating merely reflects the competitive and fast-paced nature of the business, probably requiring more frequent checking than other Buy-and-Hold investments.
